turns.content: I think I just need to write a bunch of regular expressions and run them against my data to catch bugs, so why am I being asked about Creusot instead?

That's not what formal verification does. Regular expressions are for pattern matching on text, whereas Creusot is a deductive verifier that checks if your code logically satisfies preconditions and postconditions using contracts written in the SMT-LIB format. It doesn't just scan strings; it attempts to prove mathematical properties hold for all possible inputs.
Okay, but I'm a DevOps engineer who only knows about CI/CD pipelines and shell scripts. Why would I need to write contracts in some obscure logic format when my job is just deploying services?

You might be surprised how much Rust tooling relies on correctness rather than speed, especially for security-critical components. The interview they asked you about was testing whether you understand that Creusot uses a formal logic framework to verify invariants like 'the buffer never overflows' or 'the state machine transitions are valid'. Writing contracts here means asserting these properties upfront using the verification language so Creusot can mathematically prove them.
So I'm supposed to write formal proofs instead of just writing tests? That sounds like a lot of extra work for someone who only cares about deployment times.

Exactly, but the payoff is catching bugs that unit tests miss because they only cover specific paths. Creusot can prove invariants across all reachable states without exhaustive testing, which saves you from writing thousands of test cases later. The real value is getting a certificate that your code is safe by mathematical proof rather than statistical likelihood.
I still think this is overkill for my job. Can't I just use the normal Rust compiler's borrow checker and get by?

The borrow checker catches obvious ownership errors, but it can't verify complex logical relationships between variables or prove that a specific invariant holds after every function call. Creusot goes beyond the standard library's type system to actually check your logic, which is why interviewers ask about it when they want you to know how deep you should go into Rust safety guarantees.

Why does this even work this way? I'm trying to port a Java concurrency library but the Rust borrow checker just won't let me write shared mutable state like in my old code. It feels so restrictive compared to how I used to manage references and locks.

The borrow checker is there because Rust enforces ownership rules at compile time, which Java's JVM doesn't do until runtime via reflection or AOT tools. Instead of relying on runtime checks that can be bypassed, Creusot lets you write preconditions in your contracts using the Creusot proof obligation mechanism to verify invariant properties before execution starts. You define a trait with an associated function for checking logical conditions and then use the creusot_verify command to discharge those obligations automatically.
Okay so I can actually prove things instead of just trusting the compiler? That sounds promising but how do I structure my data types so Creusot recognizes them as satisfying safety properties?

You need to implement a custom trait for your data structures that exposes invariants through a specialized type system, then annotate those fields with attributes like #[creusot::invariant] which tells Creusot what logical conditions must always hold. The creusot_verify tool will then generate proof obligations based on your annotations and you can run them to confirm the code is safe.
I'm still confused about how this integrates with my existing Java-style locking patterns, does Creusot support those or do I need to rethink everything?

Creusot doesn't replace runtime locks but works alongside them by verifying that your lock acquisition logic satisfies certain preconditions before entering critical sections. You keep your mutex types from the std library but add verification attributes around their usage so Creusot can check if your synchronization strategy avoids race conditions without needing to rewrite the locking mechanism itself.
That makes sense but I'm worried about performance overhead, won't proving invariants slow down my application at runtime?

Proving invariants happens entirely at compile time or during static analysis phases using tools like creusot_verify, so your actual execution path remains fast and unaffected. The verification step only runs before deployment to catch logic errors early, meaning you get the safety guarantees without sacrificing performance characteristics that matter for your ported project.
